title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This manuscript investigates the "label trick," a technique that facilitates the concurrent propagation of both labels and features, a characteristic beneficial to several state-of-the-art Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures. Recognizing a gap in its formal understanding, the authors undertake a theoretical analysis of this method. They begin by presenting a simplified, self-excluded variant of the label trick, achieved through an extreme splitting approach. Subsequently, the paper posits that the random splitting inherent to the label trick can be interpreted as a regularization mechanism for self-excluded label weights. Furthermore, the work explores wider applicability for the label trick, aiming to (a) enhance graph-based methodologies with trainable weight parameters and (b) mitigate the impact of stochasticity by integrating self-excluded propagation into GNNs built from linear propagation layers.

**Main Review:**
(1) The utility of the label trick in enhancing GNN performance is well-established in existing literature. The authors explicitly state their intention to bypass a re-demonstration of these known benefits, opting instead to focus on illustrating broader application contexts as outlined in Section 4. While I appreciate this objective, a thorough review of the empirical results presented in the four chosen application scenarios (Sections 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4) reveals only marginal, if any, performance gains attributable to the label trick. Indeed, when considering the standard deviation across ten independent experimental runs, the label trick often yields results that are largely on par with, or only negligibly better than, the baseline methodologies, in a significant number of cases.

(2) Building upon the preceding observation, I believe the paper could offer a more comprehensive elucidation of the label trick's precise function within GNN training. The current discussion includes a statement noting its prevalence, such as: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." While this highlights its widespread adoption, it does not inherently establish that the label trick is a direct cause of superior performance. To substantiate such a claim, an essential next step would involve conducting an ablation study, systematically comparing leading models from challenges like KDDCUP 2021 both with and without the label trick integrated. Furthermore, the paper does not adequately explore whether other advantageous characteristics, such as accelerated convergence rates or enhanced training robustness, contribute to the label trick's notable popularity.

**Summary Of The Review:**
I commend the authors for providing a rigorous theoretical framework and formal description of the label trick. Nevertheless, the practical demonstrations of novel use-cases derived from this analysis appear to be empirically unsubstantiated. Additionally, the empirical investigation into the broader impact and specific mechanisms driving the label trick's effectiveness remains somewhat incomplete.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.
reviews.main_review: The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

### Summary Of The Paper:
This paper provides a robust theoretical underpinning for the "label trick," a recent heuristic approach that involves incorporating a random subset of node labels as input features during the training phase. The authors initiate their analysis by exploring the simplest form of this trick, systematically developing a generalized formulation that frames the label trick as a regularization mechanism. Guided by this theoretical framework, the paper then proposes novel applications for the label trick in a wider range of contexts.

### Main Review:
This submission represents a highly commendable piece of research, distinguished by its rigorous theoretical development, strong conceptual justification, and innovative application of the label trick. The authors skillfully derive comprehensive formulations, effectively portraying the label trick as a regularization technique pertinent to both regression and classification tasks. The exposition of these derivations is remarkably clear and fluid, yielding several thought-provoking and profound observations and conclusions. Furthermore, the new use cases presented, which are directly informed by the theoretical insights, appear both compelling and promising, a perception strongly reinforced by the empirical evidence provided. My overall recommendation is to accept this paper, primarily owing to its exhaustive analytical depth and the significant potential it holds for inspiring broader applications. I must note, however, that my evaluation might be somewhat constrained by my specific expertise in this particular domain.

### Summary Of The Review:
A robust and intellectually stimulating theoretical contribution. It introduces innovative applications, firmly grounded in its theoretical framework, which are further substantiated by encouraging empirical results.

### Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Peer Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This paper explores the integration of labels into Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for node prediction tasks, a technique often observed in collective classification methods like label propagation. Specifically, it delves into the 'label trick,' a stochastic approach where training labels are appended to node features and subsequently masked randomly to prevent degenerate outcomes. Under simplified conditions (where node features are absent), the authors demonstrate that this process can be framed as an optimization problem minimizing both fitting and regularization components, thereby providing a theoretical basis for several proposed practical methodologies.

### Main Review

### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The core problem addressed by the authors holds significant importance, offering a valuable bridge between established label propagation techniques and modern message passing neural networks, which is a beneficial direction for the research community.

(+) The paper presents a compelling motivation for its proposed methodology, particularly in how it addresses the 'label trick's' tendency towards trivial identity mappings. The suggestion to retain only off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix, while allowing diagonal terms for feature propagation, appears to be an elegant solution, and Equation (10) is particularly intuitive in this regard.

(+) The paper effectively elaborates on the 'label trick,' acknowledging its prevalent but often heuristic application. A deeper theoretical investigation into this technique, as undertaken here, is undoubtedly a valuable academic endeavor.

(–) The authors should acknowledge and provide due credit to the work presented in https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf, which, to my knowledge, introduced the initial concept of the 'label trick.'

(–) The underlying rationale for Equation (3) requires further clarification. Specifically, why is it beneficial to represent the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying it with a weight matrix $W$? A similar request for intuitive explanation applies to the trainable smoothing component within the 'Correct & Smooth' methodology.

(–) Considering the theoretical underpinnings of this paper, it would significantly benefit from a comparative discussion with existing literature that explores label and feature propagation, even if these are not employed concurrently. For instance, a comparison with works like Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf) would be pertinent.

(–) Several equations lack clarity. The subscript $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is particularly ambiguous and hard to decipher. Equation (5) requires a more thorough explanation; specifically, the transition from the second-to-last term to the final term is not evident. Given that $D_{out}$ has a cardinality of 1, it seems the expectation should simplify to a discrete uniform sample over $i \sim \{1 \dots n\}$, resulting in $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W)]_i )$. I would appreciate a detailed explanation of what I might be overlooking in this derivation.

(–) On a broader note, the paper's overall readability is hampered by its high notational density, particularly the pervasive use of subscripts, and the somewhat disjointed presentation of 'Remarks.' A thorough revision and another round of editing would greatly enhance its clarity.

(–) For the regression tasks presented in Table 2, employing the $R^2$ metric would be a more appropriate evaluation measure.

(–) Section 4.1 is currently underdeveloped and requires substantially more detail. Key aspects such as the training procedure and the precise definition of the final model (e.g., whether it's simply supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix) are unclear. Furthermore, the discussion would benefit from references to other research that focuses on learning or modifying adjacency/propagation matrices.

### Questions:

*   Could the authors clarify why the deterministic version of the 'label trick' was omitted from the comparison in the right panel of Figure 1? Additionally, the specific GNN architecture employed in Section 5.3 appears to be unspecified and should be identified.
*   Remark 4 states that if regularization is disabled, there's 'no chance for overfitting.' This assertion requires further explanation, as the absence of regularization typically increases the risk of overfitting.
*   Given that the theoretical derivation demonstrates a potential decomposition involving a regularization term in the deterministic scenario, was this particular regularization strategy incorporated into the experiments reported in Table 3? If not, the rationale for its exclusion should be provided.

### Minor Editorial Suggestions:

*   There is a minor typographical error in Remark 2, where 'insure' should be corrected to 'ensure.'
*   An inconsistency in notation is observed in Equations (4) and (5) regarding the mixed use of parentheses and square brackets; standardizing this would improve clarity.

**Summary Of The Review:**
While the paper presents notable strengths and addresses a problem of considerable importance, it necessitates substantial revisions to fully resolve the various points raised throughout this review.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

reviews.text:
**Rewritten Review**

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This paper investigates the "label trick," a technique employed in graph-based semi-supervised learning (GSSL). In GSSL, the objective is to infer labels for unlabeled data points, given a subset of labeled points, by leveraging a graph structure where nodes represent data points and edges reflect their similarity. Traditionally, this is achieved either by diffusing known labels across the graph using methods like the graph Laplacian or by training Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) that learn parameters based on node features within the graphical model. The authors propose that the label trick effectively integrates both label information and feature-based graph modeling simultaneously.

The core of the label trick involves using known labels as input features for a GNN. To prevent the network from learning a trivial identity mapping from input labels to output labels, a dropout strategy is applied, where the true label for a given node is intentionally hidden during the training process. The paper aims to elucidate the underlying mechanisms of this label trick by examining it through simplified models and exploring its potential for novel applications.

**Main Review:**

**Strengths:**
*   The paper commendably attempts to demystify a technique that might otherwise appear as heuristic "wizard engineering," providing a more accessible understanding of the label trick through the lens of basic models.
*   The manuscript is generally well-structured and easy to comprehend, contributing to a pleasant reading experience.

**Weaknesses:**
*   The presented findings are not entirely convincing. The foundational background and motivations for the study require more thorough explanation. Furthermore, the proposed duality between the stochastic nature of the label trick and deterministic regularization lacks robust, targeted experimental validation.
*   The paper exhibits a general deficit in academic rigor, which will be elaborated upon in the specific notes below.
*   Several assertions within the paper strike me as questionable. For instance, the claim that graph-based Laplacian methods do not leverage feature knowledge is problematic, as graph construction itself typically relies on feature similarity, albeit in an unsupervised manner. Moreover, if node features are inherently noisy or unreliable, which would predictably degrade GNN performance, it logically follows that a graph structure built upon these same features would also be similarly compromised for those nodes.
*   For a paper purporting to offer a theoretical explanation for the efficacy of the label trick, I found the insights gained to be limited. Key questions remain unanswered, such as why self-excluded prediction performs less effectively than the full label trick, or what specific advantages the label trick offers over GNNs incorporating Laplacian regularization (e.g., as in Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al.) or MixUp regularization (e.g., as in Lopez-Paz et al.).

**Detailed Comments:**

**Page 1:**
1.  The paper's clarity would benefit from a more direct and rigorous presentation. For example, in the introduction (line 4), conflating the smooth variation of labels and features over a graph with the concept of i.i.d. samples introduces unnecessary confusion for the reader. Regarding rigor, the abstract's mention of an "identity mapping from input to output" is imprecise. I believe the authors are referring to a transductive setting where node features (X) might be disregarded in favor of a propagation rule, which could potentially be null, leading to "label leakage" as described in Eq. (3). The notion of an "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is ill-defined in this context.

**Page 2:**
1.  A more explicit description of the graph formalism would be beneficial. For instance, clearly stating that each node is associated with an input point x in R^d and a label in R^c, from which the node feature matrix X and label matrix Y are formed. It should also be specified that the graph is undirected with weighted edges, making the adjacency matrix S symmetric.
2.  The statement "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels" needs qualification. This likelihood is dependent on the edge weights if the graph is weighted; if unweighted, this should be explicitly stated.
3.  In Eq. (1), I suspect the second F should actually be F_tr. Without this correction, the current formulation, by Pythagoras, implicitly penalizes F towards zero on testing data. While a closed-form solution and power method might still be derivable, it is crucial to clarify if Y=0 constitutes an informative label (e.g., if Y represents logits) and whether pushing F towards zero does not introduce bias into the system. Additionally, specifying that F ∈ R^(n × c) and that the minimization objective can be solved independently for each coordinate of Y ∈ R^c (assuming no constraints on F) would enhance clarity.

**Page 3:**
1.  There appears to be an error in the second paragraph with the approximation P ≈ PY. This approximation could be more accurately expressed as an equality involving an infinite sum, given that λS is a contracting operator (with convergence understood in any norm).
2.  The phrase "where […] P smooths the training labels" feels misplaced, as P is not used in the definition of F^(k). This observation would be more appropriately positioned after the formal definition of P = (1 - λ)(I - λS)^(-1).

**Page 4:**
1.  The parenthetical "model from (…)" should be removed for conciseness.
2.  A brief explanation of SGC (Simple Graph Convolution) and its relevance to the discussion would be highly valuable. Space for this could potentially be created by condensing sections 3.2 and 3.3.
3.  Regarding Eq. (3), clarification is needed on what P represents in this context. P was previously defined as a "propagation matrix"; how is it instantiated or fixed in this particular equation?

**Appendix:**
*   The appendix contains several "overboxes," indicating LaTeX compilation issues. Please review the log file to correct these formatting errors.

**References Formatting:**
*   References currently listed as "ArXiv" should be updated to their official journal or conference publication formats where applicable.

**Summary Of The Review:**
While the endeavor to provide theoretical backing for widely adopted practical techniques is commendable, I find that this paper ultimately falls short of delivering a truly satisfactory analysis of the label trick. Specifically, it fails to rigorously establish and explain the method's superiority when compared to existing alternatives. Although the research direction holds promise, I believe the current manuscript is not yet sufficiently developed for publication.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This manuscript introduces a deterministic variant of the established label trick employed in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and other graph-based frameworks. The core of this proposal lies in a novel self-exclusion operator integrated into the propagation process, designed to effectively mitigate label leakage. The authors further explore potential applications of this deterministic approach within broader label propagation contexts. The experimental section presents comparisons against standard label propagation methods, both with and without the label trick, and investigates the influence of a regularization term in the deterministic setting versus its randomized counterpart.

**Main Review:**

**Strengths:**
1.  The paper presents a relatively novel conceptual framework for GNNs by substituting the stochastic label trick with a deterministic alternative. This approach, centered on the exclusion of self-propagation, appears to be an original contribution.
2.  The theoretical demonstration in Theorem 2, which establishes an upper bound for the proposed deterministic method with respect to the cross-entropy loss, constitutes a valuable and insightful result.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **Motivation Clarity:** The rationale for transitioning from the non-deterministic label trick to a deterministic one remains somewhat unclear. Beyond potential issues related to under-sampling, the paper does not adequately address whether computational bottlenecks or other practical limitations serve as primary motivators for this deterministic shift.
2.  **Deterministic Advantage:** While the authors assert that prior uses of the label trick lack robust theoretical substantiation, the specific advantages of their deterministic approach – particularly in terms of accuracy, speed, or other performance metrics – are not sufficiently articulated or demonstrated.
3.  **Experimental Design:** The experimental validation section is somewhat perplexing. Aside from a single instance in Figure 1, a direct comparison between the proposed deterministic method and state-of-the-art randomized label tricks is largely absent. Instead, the authors concentrate on comparisons that appear less directly pertinent to their core proposal (e.g., trainable versus non-trainable models, or with label trick versus without). A clearer justification for this experimental focus is needed.
4.  **Significance of Results:** My primary concern lies with the convincing power of the experimental validation, even for the comparisons that are included (e.g., trainable label propagation versus traditional methods, and the effect of using the label trick). In several instances, the observed differences do not demonstrate a statistically significant improvement, especially when accounting for the reported standard deviations.
5.  **Missing References:** The paper overlooks several important references concerning label propagation in graphs and the integration of label data. Specifically, the following work should be considered for inclusion:
    *   Szlam, A. D., Maggioni, M., & Coifman, R. R. (2008). Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes. *Journal of Machine Learning Research, 9*(55), 1711−1739.
6.  **Algorithmic Clarity:** Several algorithms mentioned within the text are not presented with sufficient detail or self-containment, making it challenging for readers to fully comprehend or replicate them. Furthermore, a specific reference or clarification for "C&S" is required.

**Summary Of The Review:**
Despite its novel theoretical contributions, this paper presents several fundamental issues that currently preclude acceptance. The experimental validation is largely insufficient and unconvincing, failing to adequately demonstrate the practical advantages of the proposed deterministic approach. Additionally, the underlying motivation for developing a deterministic alternative to the label trick is not thoroughly discussed or justified.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.

Main Review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This submission presents a theoretical framework for imitation learning (IL) when the expert policy is deterministic. The authors demonstrate that, in this specific setting, IL can be reformulated as a reinforcement learning (RL) problem with a fixed reward function. A key finding is that this stationary reward inherently minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner's policies. The theoretical claims are accompanied by a series of empirical experiments designed to offer validation.

**Main Review:**

**Strengths:**
1.  The paper makes a significant theoretical contribution by demonstrating that imitation learning, under the assumption of deterministic expert policies, can be effectively reduced to a standard reinforcement learning problem with a stationary reward. This analysis provides a robust theoretical foundation for several contemporary IL methodologies. Furthermore, the paper establishes a novel connection between this stationary reward and the minimization of f-divergences, a concept frequently encountered in adversarial IL, specifically proving its role in minimizing total variation distance.
2.  The mathematical derivations are presented with commendable clarity, are logically well-motivated, and are straightforward for the reader to follow.
3.  The experimental design appropriately focuses on a crucial aspect of imitation learning: investigating the relationship between the quantity of available expert data and the subsequent performance of the IL algorithm.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  A notable discrepancy arises in the practical implementation. While the proposed algorithm, like many prior works, employs a "soft" reward function (e.g., 1 - min L2 distance) to mitigate reward sparsity, this choice directly contravenes the core theoretical assumption of a deterministic expert. Although this practical decision is understandable, the authors should explicitly discuss the implications of using such a soft reward on the validity and applicability of their theoretical results.
2.  The empirical evaluation, while providing some insights, feels somewhat constrained in its scope, leaving several pertinent questions unanswered. For instance, the performance of the SAC expert utilized in the experiments appears to be relatively modest across various environments. It remains unclear whether the observed patterns and conclusions would persist if the expert's performance matched the higher benchmarks reported in previous research (e.g., as seen in the Berkeley Deep RL course homework experts). Another significant omission is an investigation into how the specific training methodology of the expert influences IL performance. Different expert training schemes, such as SAC's direct action range constraints versus a Gaussian expert's post-processing action clipping, can have substantial practical impacts on IL outcomes based on my experience. I strongly recommend expanding the experimental section to address these points, thereby offering more comprehensive validation for the theoretical contributions.

**Questions:**
1.  Could the authors provide a detailed comparison between their chosen 1-min L2 distance reward function and the approach presented in Wang et al. (2019)? Specifically, please motivate the selection of the current reward and elaborate on its comparative advantages or disadvantages.
2.  Further elaboration on the deterministic expert assumption is needed. How critical is this assumption to the practical algorithm's performance, and to what extent does it hold in real-world scenarios? Is it feasible to empirically distinguish between a truly deterministic expert and a highly stochastic one given a finite dataset?

**Summary Of The Review:**
This paper offers a compelling theoretical insight, demonstrating that for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be recast as an RL problem with a stationary reward. This not only provides a theoretical underpinning for existing methods but also establishes a valuable connection to f-divergence minimization, particularly total variation distance, prevalent in adversarial IL. While the empirical findings are consistent with prior work, their limited breadth and certain methodological choices (like the soft reward function) present some concerns. Despite these limitations, the theoretical contributions are interesting and valuable to the community. Expanding the experimental validation would significantly strengthen the paper's overall impact.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
In sum, the paper seems to contain potentially interesting results. 
The overall organization and presentation can be further improved to improve the readability of the paper.

Summary Of The Paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.

Main Review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.

In sum, the paper seems to contain potentially interesting results. 
The overall organization and presentation can be further improved to improve the readability of the paper.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Summary Of The Paper:**
This manuscript introduces a novel approach for imitation learning with deterministic experts, framing it as a reduction to a reinforcement learning problem characterized by a stationary reward function. The theoretical contributions include a certification of the expert reward recovery and bounds on the total variation distance between the expert and the imitation learner, thereby establishing a connection to adversarial imitation learning paradigms. Empirical validation through experiments on continuous control tasks demonstrates the practical effectiveness of this reduction methodology.

**Main Review:**
The paper presents several potentially valuable and thought-provoking results. To further enhance its impact and clarity, I offer the following comments and suggestions:

1.  The authors predominantly use average reward Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) in their analysis. It would be beneficial if they could explicitly state the rationale behind this choice, especially considering that discounted MDPs are more commonly employed in the broader reinforcement learning literature.
2.  The meaning of the || ||_{TV} norm is not explicitly defined within the text. Clarifying this notation would significantly improve reader comprehension.
3.  Regarding Proposition 1, the paper could be improved by specifying which particular types or classes of reinforcement learning algorithms are applicable or best suited for the proposed reduction. This detail is currently somewhat vague.
4.  For improved accessibility, it would be helpful to include clear definitions for "intrinsic reward" and "extrinsic reward" early in the paper.
5.  In Section 3, the precise mechanism by which the imitation learner is able to acquire the expert policy solely from the intrinsic reward is not fully elucidated. A more detailed explanation of this learning process would be greatly appreciated.
6.  The experimental section lacks sufficient detail regarding the comparative analysis performed. As the authors focus on a reduction technique rather than introducing new algorithms, providing a thorough description of how comparisons were conducted and against which specific benchmarks would be crucial for understanding the practical implications and reproducibility.
7.  Overall, the paper's organization and presentation could be further refined. Incorporating additional background information where relevant would help to more clearly convey the core ideas and improve the overall readability of the manuscript.

**Summary Of The Review:**
In summary, this paper contains results that are quite promising. However, the overall structure and presentation could benefit from substantial improvements to enhance its readability and ensure the key concepts are communicated with maximum clarity.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.
reviews.main_review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary of the Paper:**
This manuscript investigates the problem of imitation learning, focusing on the derivation of a policy that effectively emulates expert behavior. The authors propose an approach that leverages reinforcement learning principles, constructing a stationary reward function directly from expert datasets. Through rigorous theoretical analysis, the paper demonstrates that the resulting imitation policy achieves high expected per-step intrinsic and extrinsic rewards. Furthermore, a high-probability bound is established for the discrepancy between the expert policy and the learned imitation policy. The theoretical findings are complemented by empirical evaluations conducted on continuous control tasks, where the proposed method is shown to perform comparably to existing algorithms while offering a simplified implementation.

**Main Review:**
The central premise of employing reinforcement learning as a mechanism for imitating expert behavior presents an appealing simplicity when contrasted with several other imitation learning methodologies. However, a notable concern arises regarding the inherent sparsity of the reward function utilized in this work. In the context of training reinforcement learning models, sparse rewards are well-known to introduce significant challenges for algorithm convergence and effective policy acquisition. While the theoretical link between a superior intrinsic reward and an improved extrinsic reward is established, the practical hurdle of obtaining a high-performing policy using any standard reinforcement learning algorithm under such sparse reward conditions remains considerable. This potential practical difficulty could diminish the real-world utility of the proposed method compared to alternative, more robust comparison techniques. I would appreciate a more detailed discussion or justification addressing how these challenges associated with sparse rewards are mitigated or overcome in practice.

Furthermore, from a computational perspective, sparse reward signals frequently contribute to slower convergence rates during the learning process. To provide a more comprehensive understanding of the method's practical efficiency, it would be highly beneficial to include a comparative analysis of the computational time required by different algorithms in the experimental section.

Despite these reservations, the paper is exceptionally well-structured and lucidly written, making it very accessible to readers. The theoretical findings are particularly compelling and offer a promising and insightful perspective.

**Summary of the Review:**
This paper offers a robust theoretical framework for integrating reinforcement learning into the domain of imitation learning. The presented results are novel and highlight a promising pathway to achieve imitation learning in a remarkably straightforward manner. The intrinsic reward function, derived directly from expert datasets, is notably simple to construct. By assigning a constant reward of one for actions that match demonstrated expert behavior within a given state, the approach creates an MDP problem structure that can, in principle, be tackled by any reinforcement learning algorithm. The theoretical contributions provide valuable intuition regarding the expected performance of this method, particularly concerning its expected return. The theoretical claims are adequately supported by the provided experimental results.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
